title: [DA] Ensure nsw flag is checked for StrongSIV addrecs
author: amehsan
description:
Current implementation of Strong SIV test misses two checks. First it fails to check that the AddRecs considered are nsw. Also since we are checking for signed overflow, all the numbers must be interpreted as signed. In particular BTC must be proven to be non-negative. This check is also missing.

From a large number of experiments I have run, the proposed implementation for checking non-negativity of BTC is good enough i.e. no opportunity is missed because we fail to prove non-negativity of BTC.

----------------------------------


EDIT: I dropped the BTC check from the patch as discussed on another patch.

tests.test_body:
;; Loop-carried dependence requiring edge-reversal to expose a cycle
;; in the graph.
;; void test(unsigned long n, ptr restrict a, ptr restrict b) {
;;  for (unsigned long i = 1; i < n-1; i++)
;;    a[i] = b[i] + a[i-1];
;; }

define void @test1(i64 %n, ptr noalias %a, ptr noalias %b) {
entry:
  %sub = add i64 %n, -1
  %cmp1 = icmp ult i64 1, %sub
  br i1 %cmp1, label %test1.for.body, label %for.end

test1.for.body:                                         ; preds = %entry, %test1.for.body
  %i.02 = phi i64 [ %inc, %test1.for.body ], [ 1, %entry ]
  %arrayidx = getelementptr inbounds float, ptr %b, i64 %i.02
  %0 = load float, ptr %arrayidx, align 4
  %sub1 = add i64 %i.02, -1
  %arrayidx2 = getelementptr inbounds float, ptr %a, i64 %sub1
  %1 = load float, ptr %arrayidx2, align 4
  %add = fadd float %0, %1
  %arrayidx3 = getelementptr inbounds float, ptr %a, i64 %i.02
  store float %add, ptr %arrayidx3, align 4
  %inc = add i64 %i.02, 1
  %cmp = icmp ult i64 %inc, %sub
  br i1 %cmp, label %test1.for.body, label %for.end

for.end:                                          ; preds = %test1.for.body, %entry
  ret void
}












;; Forward loop-carried dependence *not* causing a cycle.
;; void test2(unsigned long n, ptr restrict a, ptr restrict b) {
;;  for (unsigned long i = 1; i < n-1; i++)
;;    a[i] = b[i] + a[i+1];
;; }

  %add1 = add i64 %i.02, 1
  %arrayidx2 = getelementptr inbounds float, ptr %a, i64 %add1

  %add = fadd float %0, %1
  %arrayidx3 = getelementptr inbounds float, ptr %a, i64 %i.02

define void @test1(ptr nocapture %A, ptr nocapture %B, i32 %N) {
entry:
  %cmp9 = icmp sgt i32 %N, 0
  br i1 %cmp9, label %for.body.lr.ph, label %for.end

for.body.lr.ph:                                   ; preds = %entry
  %div = sdiv i32 %N, 2
  %bot.gep = getelementptr i32, ptr %A, i32 %div

for.body:                                         ; preds = %for.body, %for.body.lr.ph
  %i = phi i32 [ 0, %for.body.lr.ph ], [ %inc, %for.body ]
  %gep.0 = getelementptr i32, ptr %A, i32 %i
  %gep.1 = getelementptr i32, ptr %bot.gep, i32 %i
  %gep.B = getelementptr i32, ptr %B, i32 %i
  %0 = load i32, ptr %gep.0, align 4
  %1 = load i32, ptr %gep.1, align 4
  %add = add nsw i32 %1, %0
  store i32 %add, ptr %gep.B, align 4
  %inc = add nsw i32 %i, 1
  %exitcond = icmp eq i32 %inc, %N
  br i1 %exitcond, label %for.end, label %for.body

for.end:                                          ; preds = %for.body, %entry

}

entry:
  br label %loop

loop:                                             ; preds = %loop, %entry

  %i.next = add nsw i32 %i, 1
  %subscript.0 = mul i32 %i, %k

  %idx.1 = getelementptr i8, ptr %a, i32 %subscript.1
  store i8 42, ptr %idx.0, align 1
  store i8 42, ptr %idx.1, align 1
  %cond.exit = icmp eq i32 %i.next, %k
  br i1 %cond.exit, label %exit, label %loop

exit:                                             ; preds = %loop
  ret void
}

comments.author: amehsan
comments.body:
More strict dependence check results in losing some loop optimization opportunities. If this turns out to be important for real workloads, there is a solution. I have previously mentioned this elsewhere, that (broadly speaking) in DA we have two choices:
- Allow arbitrary control flow, and check for overflow (Current approach)
- Allow overflow and check for control flow.

So there is a direction to address this limitation. I will open an issue regarding this, and recover some of relevant comments that I have made about this topic.

comments.author: kasuga-fj
comments.body:
However, I have to say that, in our discussions about DA, I had been *assuming* that you were already quite familiar with it. I assumed you had spent a considerable amount of time understanding and/or developing DA, perhaps in your downstream work. Since you have strongly disagreed with me several times, I also assumed those disagreements were grounded in that level of expertise.

I have to admit that recently I’ve begun to feel that there may have been a misunderstanding regarding our respective familiarity with DA. As a result of this PR (in conjunction with previous discussions), I’m now less certain about the assumptions I had been making.

I don't want to blame anyone for being unfamiliar with something. For example, commenting on PRs is welcome, but as in case #162281, dismissing something outright from the start strikes me as overdoing it. Also, explicitly stating "objection" without sufficient experience in the topic seems a bit too much in some cases. In such a situation, I feel it becomes almost impossible to reach a consensus, as continuing the discussion without sufficient knowledge will only lead us nowhere. It may simply end up being a waste of time for everyone involved.

I think the topic of this PR is a good first issue to start with, and will help you get familiar with the codebase of DA. If you are willing to learn and understand the implementation of DA, please try reading the code and identifying the mistake of this change on your own.

comments.author: amehsan
comments.body:
> This change is clearly incorrect... I'll refrain from pointing out where the mistake is, as I think it's a "good first issue" and a useful exercise for you to get familiar with the codebase.

It's possible that there is mistake in the PR. I am not in rush for merging it and I don't mind finding it on my own.

If a reviewer believes a patch is not ready to be merged, it is their responsibility to point out the problem and defend it. A reviewer cannot prevent a patch from merging simply by claiming there exists a problem and not presenting any actionable details.

comments.author: amehsan
comments.body:
> This change is clearly incorrect...

I took a look at the code. I don't see something obviously wrong, but there might be something that I am missing. The check for nsw flag can be done inside `strongSIV` function too. But since it is eventually needed for other tests as well, I think it makes more sense to put it in `testSIV`. At the moment the check is not consistently applied to all invocation of some of the other tests, but that should not create a correctness issue. Please raise a concrete and specific objection to the patch if you see a problem.

comments.author: kasuga-fj
comments.body:
> But since it is eventually needed for other tests as well, I think it makes more sense to put it in `testSIV`.

(Forgot to mention about it)
My preference is to make the behavioral changes as small as possible. It makes it easier to review the diffs on the regression tests. We can consolidate the `nsw` check into a single place at any time.
